data.title: L'Oréal warns of Chinese ecosystem as Q3 sales miss expectations
data.description:
L'Oréal S.A. (OTCPK:LRLCY) warned that the Chinese ecosystem remains challenging, with declining sales in the region resulting in lower-than-expected sales for the French cosmetics giant in the third quarter ended September 30.

“The situation in the Chinese ecosystem has become even more challenging, but we believe in the future of this market and hope that the governmental stimulus will help improve consumer confidence,” CEO Nicolas Hieronimus said in a statement.

In June, Hieronimus made a similar warning that lingering weakness in China would impact growth in the beauty market.

Although sales growth was achieved in all regions, except North Asia, Q3 results were below expectations except for North American sales which were up 5.2% compared to +3.67% estimates. Sales in Europe increased 5.6% (+6.43% est), and in SAPMENA-SSA (which includes Australia, New Zealand, Thailand, Saudi Arabia, India, Vietnam, and Sub-Saharan Africa) where sales improved 8.0% (+12.4% est).

North Asian sales were down 6.5%, worsening from a 2.4% drop in the previous quarter and missing expectations for a gain of 2.15%.

By division, sales of Professional Products rose 6.1% (est +4.5%), followed by a 5.8% gain in Luxe sales (est +4.43%), 1.4% gain in Consumer Products (est +5.18%), and 0.8% increase in sales of Dermatological Beauty (est +11.2%), resulting in an overall sales increase of 3.4%, missing the Bloomberg estimate of +5.8%. All sales are reported as like-for-like ("LFL").

The results weighed on shares of L'Oréal (OTCPK:LRLCY) along with other names in the sector including Estée Lauder (EL), Coty (COTY), e.l.f. Beauty (ELF), and Ulta (ULTA) all of which are down by 2% to nearly 6%.

data.title: Verizon's capex increase fails to move telecom equipment stocks
data.description:
Verizon (NYSE:VZ) expects to increase its capital expenditures in 2025 above market expectations, but the news failed to move telecom equipment manufacturer stocks on Tuesday.

Verizon projected capital expenditures to range between $17B and $17.5B for all of 2024. It forecasts 2025 capex to range between $17.5B and $18.5B, which was above the estimate of $17.56B.

"I always said that if we see an opportunity where we can grow faster, and we can invest more in capex, we will explain that if we go outside the normal BAU levels," said Verizon CEO Hans Vestberg, during Verizon's third quarter 2024 earnings call on Tuesday.

"But ultimately, if we see opportunities, remember the capital allocation priorities, we spend it in business, but we also want to explain that is something additionally we can get and that we can share with our shareholders," Vestberg added.

Verizon plans to double its subscribers for wireless service over the next four years. It is investing heavily in C-band spectrum for 5G coverage. Vestberg said its revealed capex plans do not include the eventual emergence of 6G, which is not expected to arrive until around 2030.

Verizon shares were down 4% by early afternoon trading on Tuesday. Telecom equipment manufacturers, which look to benefit from Verizon's higher-than-expected 2025 capex, remained mostly muted following the news.

Ericsson (NASDAQ:ERIC) was down 0.2%, Cisco (NASDAQ:CSCO) +0.3%, Nokia Oyj (NYSE:NOK) -0.26%, Arista Networks (NYSE:ANET) -2.3%, and Juniper Networks (NYSE:JNPR) -0.1%.

data.title: Braze gets initiated with bullish views at Stifel
data.description:
Stifel began coverage of Braze (NASDAQ:BRZE) with a Buy rating and a $37 target price target.

The New York-based company provides software development kits that automatically manage data ingestion and deliver mobile and web notifications.

Analysts led by J. Lane said that as consumers have increased their digital footprints over the years since the start of mobile and social, and consumer expectations have shifted toward more personalized brand experiences, delivering personalized, mobile, and real-time experiences has become a necessity for successful brand in all industries.

The analysts see Braze as well-positioned to capture share in the customer engagement software space, and believe the company has built a leading-edge multi-channel marketing platform which is difficult to replicate.

In addition, the analysts see several ways Braze is actively lowering customer friction, and think that the company remains in its early days of capturing its addressable market.

The analysts view several things directly benefitting customer engagement providers such as Braze — forced shift from third-party data to first party data, consumer expectations and demands for personalization, digitally-native consumers and generative AI-powered marketing motions, among other things.

Personalized, digital outreach has become critical for organizations, and companies like Braze are directly serving this need, according to the analysts.

Lane and his team believe that market trends have dictated a need for a customer engagement solution like Braze for nearly all business-to-consumer, or B2C, organizations, and believe these trends are becoming increasingly important for the B2B businesses.

data.title: Paul Tudor Jones sees Treasury selloff, with debt to soar under either Trump or Harris
data.description:
Billionaire hedge fund manager Paul Tudor Jones said he’s staying away from owning fixed-income under a White House run by either candidates in the 2024 presidential election as the country’s fiscal position looks set to deteriorate.

The Tudor Investment Corporation founder warned about the U.S.’s steepening debt trajectory, and he and backed some inflation trades.

“We’re going to be broke really quickly unless we get serious about dealing with our spending issues,” Jones told CNBC in an interview Tuesday. “I don't know if we'll be able to cut spending that much. 60% of our spending are transfer payments,” which can include entitlements such as Social Security benefits.

Both former President Donald Trump and Vice President Kamala Harris in their White House runs are pitching hefty spending plans and “handing out tax cuts like their Mardi Gras beads,” which, if enacted, would swell the budget deficit and drive up debt, he said.

“The Treasury market won't tolerate it,” Jones said, suggesting the potential for a selloff in the bond market (US10Y)(US30Y)(US2Y). After this election, “will we have a Minsky moment where all of a sudden there's a point of recognition that … what they're talking about is actually fiscally impossible, financially impossible?”

The U.S. government’s annual budget deficit rose by $138B to $1.8T in the fiscal year that ended in September, federal officials said this month. The FY24 estimate was 6.4% of gross domestic product, up from 6.2% in fiscal 2023.

“I am clearly not going to own any fixed income, and I'm going to be short the back end of fixed income because it's just completely the wrong price,” Jones said.

The hedge fund boss voiced skepticism about betting markets projecting a Trump win of the Oval Office. But he’s positioning for a Trump victory because polling data indicate improvement for his prospects. “It just means more inflation trades,” Jones said.

Ultimately, “all roads lead to inflation” for the U.S. economy after the 2024 election, regardless of the winner, Jones said.

“I'm long gold (XAUUSD:CUR), I'm long bitcoin (BTC-USD),” he said. “I think commodities are so ridiculously under owned, so I'm long commodities,” he said, adding that he would also use the Nasdaq (COMP:IND)(NDX) as an inflation hedge. “And … zero fixed income," Jones said.

Investors interested in tracking the markets via ETFs, here are some Nasdaq-related funds: (QQQ), (TQQQ) and (SQQQ), and some S&P 500 (SP500) funds: (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SH), and (SPXU).

A few gold-related funds: (GLD), (IAU), (BAR), and (DUST). Here are some bitcoin-backed ETFs: (GBTC), (IBIT), (ARKB), (BTCO), and (FBTC).

Readers interested in investing topics tied to the upcoming election can find more information at the upcoming Seeking Alpha Investing Forum: Election 2024; please visit the event website.

data.title: Amazon-backed Anthropic's AI tool Claude aims to use computers like humans
data.description:
Anthropic said the new version of its AI model Claude can now potentially emulate the way people interact with their computers.

Anthropic — which is backed by Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) and Alphabet's (GOOGL) (NASDAQ:GOOG) Google — said the latest version of Claude 3.5 Sonnet can follow a user's commands to move a cursor around their computer's screen, click on relevant locations, and input information via a virtual keyboard. Thus emulating the way people interact with their own computer.

The skill is currently in public beta mode.

The company trained Claude to interpret what is happening on a screen and then use the software tools available to carry out tasks.

Anthropic noted that computer use is an entirely different approach to AI development. Until now, large language model, or LLM, developers made tools to fit the model, producing custom environments where AIs use designed tools to complete tasks. "Now, we can make the model fit the tools — Claude can fit into the computer environments we all use every day," said the company in a press release.

The AI startup — which is on track to generate $1B in revenue this year — said its goal for Claude is to take pre-existing pieces of computer software and simply use them as a person would.

Anthropic found that the Claude 3.5 Sonnet, including its new computer use skill, remains at AI Safety Level 2 — which means that the AI model does not need higher standards of safety and security measures than those the company currently has.

One concern the company identified was 'prompt injection' — a type of cyberattack where malicious instructions are given to an AI model, causing it to either override its prior directions or perform unintended actions which deviate from the user's original intent.

Anthropic said that since Claude can interpret screenshots from computers connected to the internet, it is possible that it may be exposed to content that includes prompt injection attacks.

Anthropic acknowledged that there is still a lot to do. "Even though it’s the current state of the art, Claude’s computer use remains slow and often error-prone," the

Anthropic's AI product Claude has grown in popularity. Amazon's updated Alexa, reportedly, will be powered mainly by Anthropic's AI models Claude rather than the tech giant's own AI,

Claude, amid the crowded LLM market, competes with OpenAI's ChatGPT for both enterprise and consumer markets. Meanwhile, other tech heavyweights, such as Microsoft, are also trying to woo enterprise customers with their AI offerings.

On Monday, Microsoft announced that it would allow businesses to start making their own autonomous AI agents from next month. Rival Salesforce (CRM) had already introduced its own configurable AI models in September.

data.title: Dye & Durham said to consider sale after takeover interest: report
data.description:
• Canadian legal software company Dye & Durham (OTCPK:DYNDF) (TSX:DND:CA) is discussing a sale of the company after receiving interest from several parties, Bloomberg reported.
  • Toronto-based Dye & Durham, which provides software to law firms and banks, has been approached by private equity firms and strategic companies, the news outlet added, citing people with knowledge of the matter. The process is still at an early stage and a deal may not come to fruition, Bloomberg added.
  • Dye & Durham went public in 2020 and acquired several companies, but concerns over its leverage ratio put a damper in that strategy.
  • Shares closed at C$16.90 on Monday, valuing the company at C$1.13B.
  • Dye & Durham did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Wall Street set for two-day losing streak for the first time since early September
data.description:
Wall Street's benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) on Tuesday was headed for its first back-to-back losing session since early September, as sentiment was weighed down by uncertainty over the Federal Reserve's pace of rate cuts and caution exactly two weeks ahead of the U.S. presidential election.

The S&P (SP500) was last -0.17% at 5,843.89 points in afternoon trade, and was on track for a second straight negative day for only the first time since posting a four-day losing streak in the first week of September. Meanwhile, the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.08% at 18,524.37 points, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.02% at 42,939.86 points.

Of the 11 S&P sectors, seven were in the red, led by Industrials. Consumer Staples topped the four gainers.

Traders have been recalibrating their expectations for aggressive Fed rate cuts lately after economic data has come in stronger than anticipated. Central bank speakers Minneapolis Fed president Neel Kashkari and Kansas City Fed president Jeffrey Schmid on Monday expressed a preference for gradual policy easing.

Market participants responded to the clouding of the rate cut outlook by dumping bonds, which drove the benchmark 10-year U.S. Treasury yield (US10Y) over 4.20% for the first time since late-July. On Tuesday, the instrument was up nearly 1 basis point to 4.21%. The shorter-end 2-year yield (US2Y) was up about 3 basis points to 4.07%.

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.

Tuesday's spotlight was also largely focused on the earnings season. Investors received quarterly reports from some of the most well-known U.S. firms in the world.

"It seems that a touch of uncertainty over the next major move for equities has crept in this week. If investors hear some decent third quarter updates today, along with some positive forward guidance, then markets could quickly turn positive once again," David Morrison, senior market analyst at Trade Nation, said.

"But the VIX has popped up this morning. Add in higher yields and uncertainty ahead of an election which remains too close to call, then it’s possible that equities may have to pull back a bit further before investors feel confident enough to add to their exposure," Morrison added.

Some of today's notable earnings-related moves include a +9.2% surge in General Motors (GM), with the stock also topping the percentage gainers on the S&P 500 (SP500). Strong sales of the legacy carmaker's traditional internal combustion engine vehicles led to a 16% climb in Q3 North American revenue.

Philip Morris International (PM) swelled +8.7% and was another top S&P percentage gainer. The cigarette giant upped its annual profit guidance on solid demand for its IQOS heated tobacco products and its ZYN nicotine pouches.

Conversely, GE Aerospace (GE) slid -8.2% and was among the top percentage losers on the S&P. The maker of jet engines missed quarterly revenue expectations.

Dow 30 component Verizon (VZ) was -4.0% after its quarterly revenue came in below consensus.

Semiconductor bellwether Texas Instruments (TXN) will be announcing earnings after the closing bell.

data.title: JPMorgan Chase chief Dimon said to be open to a Harris administration role
data.description:
• JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) Chairman and CEO Jamie Dimon has reportedly told associates in private that he might consider a role in a Harris administration, possibly Treasury Secretary. For almost a year, media reports have speculated on the potential for Dimon to become the next head of the U.S. Treasury.
  • Publicly, Dimon hasn't endorsed a candidate in the presidential race, considering the potential repercussions should Donald Trump win a second term, the New York Times reported, citing three people with knowledge of Dimon's private conversations.
  • Late last year, Axios reported that the long-time JPMorgan chief would be considered for Treasury Secretary in a second Trump administration. Then in July, Donald Trump denied that he was considering him or BlackRock's Larry Fink for the role.
  • Earlier this month, Dimon said the chance of his joining an administration is almost nil. Still, he said he's an "American patriot" and that his "country is more important to me than my company."
  • Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
  • Readers interested in investing topics tied to the upcoming election can find more information at the upcoming Seeking Alpha Investing Forum: Election 2024; please visit the event website.

data.title: Teva, Alvotech get expanded FDA approval for Stelara biosimilar
data.description:
Teva Pharmaceutical Industries (NYSE:TEVA) and Alvotech (NASDAQ:ALVO) said the FDA has expanded labeling for its biosimilar of Johnson & Johnson’s (JNJ) Stelara to include the treatment of Crohn’s disease and ulcerative colitis.

The companies said the FDA approved a 130mg/26mL single dose vial of the product for intravenous infusion, which should further align the label of its biosimilar Selarsdi with Stelara’s indications.

In April, the FDA approved 45mg/0.5mL and 90mg/mL single-dose prefilled syringes of Selarsdi for subcutaneous injection for the treatment of plaque psoriasis and psoriatic arthritis.

The product is expected to be launched in the U.S. for all indications in Q1 2025, according to a statement.

Teva and Alvotech launched a biosimilar of Humira in the U.S. in May.

data.title: Regeneron drops after court rejects request for temporary injunction for Eylea generic
data.description:
• Regeneron Pharmaceutical (NASDAQ:REGN) fell 2.4% after a court rejected its request for a temporary injunction blocking sales of its generic Eylea.
  • Marketed globally by Regeneron (REGN) and its German partner Bayer (OTCPK:BAYZF), Eylea has generated $5.7B in sales for the company in the U.S. in 2023.
  • Developing story ...
